sentences.text: Yes, yes.
They told us to undo all the clothing, because the Germans knew it, that Jewish people got money.
And they got jewelry.
And they got diamonds, whatever they have-- to tear them apart.
And they used to bring us suitcases so-- so we can put it in.
Separate the diamonds.
Separate the gold.
And separate the money.

And we used to stack those things-- those jewelries, and diamonds, and money-- hundreds and hundreds of suitcases every day. 'Cause when they burned the people, they used to bring to us the-- the clothing.
I was doing this, Pauline, for one year.
Can you imagine how many diamonds, and how many gold, and how many-- how much money I used to pick?
Not only me, I mean, we were quite a few women there that we were doing-- day after day.
I used to play with the diamonds.
I didn't care about them.
I didn't want them.
It didn't mean nothing to me, because the suffering was enough.
But we used to packed every day hundreds of suitcases.
It was not pleasant.
But you had to do it.
If somebody put a gun on your-- on your back, on your head, and-- and-- and tell you you have to do it, you do it.
You want to save yourself.
That's what-- that was-- we were doing there for one year in this-- in the-- Auschwitz, just where-- where the crematoriums was.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
They didn't know where they're going.
They didn't.
But we did.
And it was breaking my heart.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And after what we did that, besides that, we tore apart the clothing.
We used to put the men clothing separate, women separate, children separate.
And we stacked one on top of the other.
And this be sent in over as well.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everything has been going over.
In here.
In-- underneath the--
--hem-- all at here-- children's and men's, because they wanted to-- to-- to-- the lapels, you know, anywhere they could find a-- a place, they used to put it there.
I didn't-- I didn't believe it exist so much diamonds in my life.
I didn't-- I didn't dream it was so much gold in my life.
I was young.
I never saw this before.
And one day, they ask me if I want to be a kapo.
A kapo means that you have to look after the others to do the right work, to do the right job, not to slow down, to do the right thing.
And I said to him, I can't do it.
Because kapo has to be strong and to hit people to do the-- the job.
I can't do it.
They are my people.
I cannot do it.
So choose somebody else.
There was a block-- a block-- the same block that I was living in Birkenau-- the same block-- blocks.
There were blocks.
This-- you know?
The same types of blocks-- and again, without mattress and without blankets-- only wooden beds.
The same thing.
The same food-- never too much-- always soup and a piece of bread.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
The same.
The same thing like mine.
We all were together.
We all suffered together.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS]

[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] To do this day after day, that was murder-- murder-- to see so beautiful children passing through and waving to us, you know, through the window, because we couldn't go out; and mothers; and so beautiful people.
And we knew they're going.
And it was break heart.
It was too much for me.
It was too much for anybody to take.
A year.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
After all of that what I've been through, what we did for a year, we heard-- I don't know if we heard or not-- something was talking about it-- that the Russians and American start to come closer.
So they took us from there, because they needed us to work.
We were young women.
You know, they needed us.
And they took us.
And-- and they took us out of this brick.
I don't know how this brick.
I don't know what you call it this-- like another camp-- Ravensbruck.
That's it.
I forgot the name-- Ravensbruck.
They took us there, all-- the whole people what-- what were there.
And they told us that we will be here.
They didn't told us anything that-- it was not important to talk to us.
We have to do it what they want us to do.
We walk-- always walk-- nothing any more with trains or anything like that.
We walk there.
I don't remember exactly, Pauline.
But we walk this-- in this Ravensbruck.
And we were there maybe for-- for about three-- three weeks.
It was spring-- spring.
It was not very cold.
It was spring.
They took--
Yes, yes, before the-- the war.
Anyway, so when-- we were there for three weeks.
Then that was not good enough, because the-- the Americans and the-- and the Russian, they were coming closer.
So they-- they took away from us as well from there.
And we were on a march.
Nothing.
Nothing.
For three weeks we did do nothing, because it was for them too short time to do anything.
Yes, we heard voices already.
Somebody was telling that because the Russians are coming.
The Americans coming.
You know why we were very happy?
Because we thought maybe we have a chance to live.
And then when we went on a march--
Yes, yes, we all went to the march.
And-- and there were women, Germans, you know, dress up with the soldiers, German soldiers, that march with us on the march.
One of them came to me, and she said, don't call me anymore by my name.
They used to call them [NON-ENGLISH]-- I don't know the name for them.
Don't call me.
She say, call me by my name.
And I got a little bit-- on this-- on this-- I-- I understood a little bit that something is going on.
She's asking me not to call her by a name.
Otherwise, I-- I have to respect her.
So I knew something is going on.
Anyway, I want to be alive.
I want to live.
I'll do anything.
I will call her anything just to be alive.
We were marching.
But the time was short-- very short.
A few days.
A few days.
They used to give again the soup with a piece of bread.
That-- that was the pattern.
That was the only pattern they give us.
And on the march-- I don't know, it was looking that it was short of time.
I don't know what was happening.
We didn't know anything about it.
We only knew there was the dogs with the guns, and behind us.
Then they knew it to each other that something has to be done.
They start to shoot the-- the people-- to shoot on us.
The Germans, the German soldiers start to shoot us before we are liberated, and start to shoot.
I was lucky I skip.
I saw shooting.
Many other-- for-- like me, when they saw shooting, everybody start to-- to-- to run away.
No, only women.
Never men with us.
I don't know.
I wouldn't have a clue.
I saw-- I-- I want to go away from-- from the-- from the [NON-ENGLISH].
What you call this-- the gun.
I want to run away from the gun.
So I was running.
I saw other people run.
So I ran as well.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And all young women, all young girls.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I-- I wouldn't have a clue where I was.
We were marching.
I don't know where-- where I was!
I don't know the places.
I wouldn't have a clue.
You can't image.
You just can't image.
I said to myself, it's really that I'm going to be alive?
That I going to tell the people what I've been through?
Is this possible?
Again, I said, God must love me to let me live.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Then we-- I saw people running from one place to the other.
Everybody was crazy.
They didn't know where they were running to.
It was Belgiums.
It was from different-- all countries.
I didn't want to go back to Greece anymore, because I knew it that I haven't got my parents anymore.
I knew they all were dead.
Only I knew that my sister-- they told me when I was in-- in Auschwitz-- they told me that she escaped.
And I was very happy for her.
I said to myself, at least she will be one to be alive.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I-- I-- I was crying.
And that man said to me, look.
Don't worry, I'll tell that you are my cousin.
I'll get you through.
And I haven't-- I haven't got-- I don't know the language.
I haven't got no money.
I've got nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I said, thank you very much.
I was in her place for a while.
One day, she came, and she said to me, you know, people came from concentration camp.
They're giving money.
They give them clothing.
I can do this for you to bring for you.
But you have to give me your parents' name.
No, no.
I'd been through so much.
And I was scared to say I'm Jewish.
I suffer because I was Jewish.
I never done anything wrong to anybody in my life.
Why should I suffer like that?
But I told her.
I was scared.
And I said to her, no.
I'm not Jewish.
But afterwards, when she came with a proposition that she wants to give me money and clothing for the people came from-- from concentration camp-- I was so young and naive.
And I told my father's name is Israel.
And she knew straight away that I'm not Jewish.
That I was Jewish-- sorry-- that I was Jewish.
And she said to me, in that case, she said, I don't want you here.
She went to the Greek consulate.
And she said to me-- she said to them, this girl is here without papers, and-- and she is-- and she is not allowed to stay here.
You have to send her back to Greece.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And then, I-- I went to-- to the street, and I was crying.
A man came-- approach me.
I don't know the man-- who he was.
He say, come with me.
I introduce you to my wife.
And I didn't know that he's a Jewish man.
I didn't know.
And I went there-- introduced me to his wife.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she was very nice to me.
And she knew the situation.
And she said, don't worry.
You can stay with us.
We're going to the consulate.
And we going to-- what do you call it-- be responsible for you.
Because we are Belgians, born here.
And by going to them, telling that we are responsible for you, they will let you stay.
And so they did.
And I-- I was living there.
What-- I was working there for the-- I-- I said to them, I don't want any money.
I only want to stay here, to-- feed me.
And I'll do anything.
I didn't-- I didn't even want not-- not even a cent.
I didn't have a cent on my-- on my pocket.
I didn't want any money.
I only want to have a roof above my head.
And I want to have food enough to eat.
One day, my husband, that I married, came in.
It was a nephew from those people, for those nice people-- a nephew.
And I don't know.
We fell in love.
Soon we saw each other, we fell-- he was in concentration camp as well.
He was Polish.
And he-- he suffer as much as I did-- but not in mine camp-- in different camp.
And we fell in love.
And we married in Belgium.
You got a photo there that you saw before.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We came here-- my husband, he had family from before the war.
And they send us permit to come here.
But my husband came first, before me, one year.
We married there.
And he had a permit, because-- I met him after-- afterwards he had a permit, because the family sent for-- only for him.
And then when I married, he came here.
And he sent a permit for me.
And I was a year living with them.
And then I came here to this beautiful country.
Australia was very good to us.
I thank Australia with all my heart to give us this opportunity to make a home, a good home.
They were wonderful.
It's a-- it's a country that no many countries in the world will give us this opportunity.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My husband worked very hard.
He was a baker.
His father was a baker.
That is for generation.
He was a baker.
And he was working very hard.
And we did-- things got well.
And then I had two children-- a girl and a boy.
Greta is her name, the oldest one.
And she got two children-- Loni and Troy, and a husband, Ben.
They're all very happy.
And I'm very happy for them.
I'm very proud of them.
Harry got three children-- Jarrod, Kiarra, and Tynan.
They are good kids; and Diane, his wife.
I'm really-- I've got a family of my own that I didn't have that before.
I'm so proud.
I'm so happy.
I really am.
For the first time in my life, I can say I'm really happy.
And my husband died when-- in 1989-- from a heart attack.
He was a wonderful man-- very clever man.
He provided us with all the good things that we have today.
He work hard for this family.
And I'm sorry to see him go so quickly.
We had two bar mitzvahs.
He was none of them there.
We had a bar mitzvah for Troy-- for Jarrod first.
He was older; then Troy.
And he was not there to see it.
Then after that, six years-- I was on my own for 1 and 1/2 years.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was sad to be on your own.
It's not good to be on your own.
Especially when you've been through so much, you want somebody near you to talk to.
It's very hard to be on your own.
I met Gary-- a nice man.
He knew my husband.
He knew him in-- from Belgium, he knew him.
His wife passed away for-- she died from cancer.
But I'm very lucky to find a man like him.
He's very generous.
He's kind, understanding, thoughtful.
Every woman should have a man like that.
You mean my story?
I did told them-- not everything.
This is the first time in my life that-- I didn't want to do this interview, because it's too much pain for me-- too painful.
But believe you me, I am glad I did it.
Because my pain in my heart was too-- for too long-- so many years to keep inside me.
And I'm very glad to share it with you and for the world to know.
This is not just a story.
This is the real truth.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I tell you another story.
I came here to Australia.
And the first time in my life that I went to see a movie, a movie that's for-- in concentration camp.
I wish I wouldn't go, because it's very hard for me to see what I've been through.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were sitting and watching.
And behind us it was sitting a Australian couple-- a wife and her husband.
And when the picture was being shown, this lady couldn't stop crying.
She crying and crying-- cried to see what they were showing in Auschwitz, in-- in the concentration camp.
The husband said to her, darling, don't cry.
It's only a movie.
When the picture finished, a friend of ours turn around, and he said to the gentleman, he said, this was not a movie.
This is what the true story, because we were in it.
The man didn't say a word-- not one word.
And what I wanted to say is the world should know this is not our real story.
This is the truth-- only the truth.
And I hope that one day people will realize that is the truth-- and-- and not to let it happen again.
Because we are in a wonderful would.
We have the right to exist.
Everybody has the right to exist.
You mean before or now?
I'm enjoying my life.
I'm happy, because I have a family.
I'm not alone anymore.
You see, I-- I-- what I want to say is they took away from me my youth, my freedom, my mother, that I wanted so much to have it.
They took away from me my best years of my life.
A young girl, 16, needs a mother, needs parents, needs the-- the-- the-- freedom.
I didn't have that.
They took away from me everything.
The best years of my life, they took away from me.
